front: What is the primary difference between Memory-Mapped I/O and Isolated I/O?
back: Memory-Mapped I/O uses the same address space for both memory and I/O devices, so the CPU uses memory access instructions for I/O. Isolated I/O (or Port-Mapped I/O) uses a separate, dedicated address space and requires special I/O instructions (e.g., IN and OUT on x86).

front: What are the three main methods for performing I/O data transfers?
back: 1. Programmed I/O (Polling): The CPU repeatedly checks the status of an I/O device. 2. Interrupt-Driven I/O: The I/O device interrupts the CPU when it is ready to transfer data. 3. Direct Memory Access (DMA): A dedicated DMA controller manages the data transfer between I/O device and main memory without continuous CPU involvement.

front: What is the key advantage of using Direct Memory Access (DMA)?
back: It offloads the data transfer task from the CPU. The DMA controller handles the movement of data between an I/O device and memory, freeing the CPU to execute other tasks and significantly improving system efficiency and performance.

front: In Interrupt-Driven I/O, what is an interrupt service routine (ISR)?
back: An ISR is a special software routine (like a function) that the CPU executes in response to a hardware interrupt from an I/O device. It handles the data transfer and any necessary processing before returning the CPU to its previous task.

front: What is the main disadvantage of Programmed I/O (Polling)?
back: It is highly inefficient. The CPU wastes a large number of cycles simply waiting and repeatedly checking the status of the I/O device, a process known as 'busy-waiting'. This CPU time could be used for other productive work.
